empirical_sampling.note: 1%-row sampling was unsuitable (breaks per-order aggregation); full scan used instead for correctness

join_order.rationale: Decorrelate IN subquery: single pass to build compact 624-entry hash set

join_order.build: qualified_orders hash_set (624 entries, fits in L1 cache)
join_order.rationale: Probe 15M orders against 624-entry set; late-materialize o_custkey/o_orderdate/o_totalprice for only 624 hits

join_order.build: customer_custkey_hash (pre-built mmap index, zero build cost)
join_order.rationale: Only 624 lookups — trivially fast with pre-built hash index

join_order.aggregate: SUM(l_quantity) per qualifying order using positions from index
join_order.output: sum_qty per qualifying order (~624 × avg 4.8 lineitem rows = ~3000 row accesses)
join_order.rationale: Avoids re-scanning 60M lineitem rows in Phase 2; index gives direct row positions per orderkey

late_materialization.rationale: Scan only o_orderkey (60MB) across 15M rows; fetch o_custkey/o_orderdate/o_totalprice via random access only for 624 qualifying rows — avoids loading 3 additional columns for 15M−624 non-qualifying rows

data_structures.phase1_aggregation: compact_hash_map<int32_t, double>(pre_sized_16M) — thread_local partial maps merged after parallel scan; 15M distinct keys, open-addressing linear probing
data_structures.qualified_orders_set: compact_hash_set<int32_t>(capacity=2048) — 624 entries, fits entirely in L1 cache (2048 × 4 bytes = 8KB), O(1) probe with near-zero cache miss
data_structures.phase2_result_buffer: flat_vector<ResultRow>(pre_sized_1024) — 624 result rows, struct-of-arrays layout for sort key extraction
data_structures.aggregation: flat_vector_direct — 624 groups, no hash table needed; group identity = o_orderkey (unique per order)

phase1_lineitem_scan.merge_cost: O(60M) total insertions across 64 maps — linear and cache-friendly
phase1_lineitem_scan.rationale: 60M rows × 12 bytes = 720MB; parallel scan with thread-local maps avoids all contention; merge is O(60M) total

phase2_orders_scan.synchronization: thread_local_merge — 624 qualifying rows total, trivially merged
phase2_orders_scan.rationale: 15M × 4 bytes = 60MB (o_orderkey only); probe against L1-cached 624-entry set; output 624 qualifying indices

phase2_index_lookups.rationale: Only 624 lookups into customer_custkey_hash and lineitem_orderkey_hash — parallelism overhead exceeds benefit

topk_sort.rationale: 624 rows is trivially small; std::partial_sort(624, K=100) completes in microseconds

index_usage.purpose: For each of 624 qualifying orderkeys, retrieve positions[] and count to sum l_quantity without re-scanning 60M lineitem rows
index_usage.layout: uint32_t num_positions=59986052, positions[59986052], uint32_t cap=33554432, HEntry32{int32_t key; uint32_t offset; uint32_t count} × 33554432
index_usage.estimated_accesses: 624 hash probes + ~3000 random row accesses to l_quantity.bin

index_usage.purpose: Lookup c_name row position for each of 624 qualifying orders via o_custkey
index_usage.layout: uint32_t num_positions=1500000, positions[1500000], uint32_t cap=4194304, HEntry32 × 4194304
index_usage.estimated_accesses: 624 hash probes + 624 random reads into c_name.bin (fixed 26-byte slots)

scan_strategy.phase1_lineitem: single_pass_fused — read l_orderkey + l_quantity together, accumulate into thread-local hash maps in one pass
scan_strategy.phase2_orders: single_pass_late_materialization — first pass o_orderkey only; random-access fetch of o_custkey/o_orderdate/o_totalprice for 624 hits
scan_strategy.phase2_customer_lineitem: index_driven — skip full scans entirely; use pre-built mmap hash indexes for 624 point lookups

memory_pattern.phase1: streaming_sequential — l_orderkey and l_quantity read sequentially in 50K-row morsels; thread-local hash maps fit in L3 per thread
memory_pattern.phase2_orders: streaming_sequential for o_orderkey scan; random_access_with_prefetch for 624 deferred column fetches
memory_pattern.phase2_index: random_access — mmap hash index probes + random row accesses; 624 × ~5 rows = ~3000 accesses, all likely in OS page cache after Phase 1

phase_breakdown.phase1_lineitem_scan_720MB_parallel: ~80ms (64 threads, ~720MB memory-resident via OS page cache, ~9GB/s effective bandwidth)
phase_breakdown.phase2_orders_scan_60MB_parallel: ~15ms (15M × 4 bytes = 60MB, o_orderkey only, semi-join probe against L1-cached 624-entry set)

comparison.target: beat DuckDB by exploiting: (1) L1-cached 624-entry semi-join set, (2) mmap pre-built indexes eliminating Phase 2 lineitem re-scan, (3) late materialization on orders reducing I/O by 75%
